title: Kremlin suspected of flying drones over Europe using Russian shadow fleet
gist: An IISS report uses AIS maritime tracking to link 144 mysterious drone sightings over European military bases, critical infrastructure, and airports (Aug 2024-Feb 2026) to Russian 'shadow fleet' oil tankers launching low-cost drones, exposing a fragmented European air-defense and maritime-accountability gap that the EU's counter-drone system won't close until end of 2027.

insights.claim: An IISS assessment attributes a coordinated Kremlin drone campaign over Europe to Russian-linked commercial ships and 'shadow fleet' vessels, using AIS maritime tracking data showing these ships were frequently located near drone incidents across a dozen NATO countries and Ireland between August 2024 and February 2026.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Signals a durable state-sponsored gray-zone threat vector against European infrastructure; watch defense-spend, counter-drone, and maritime-surveillance budgets as a multi-year tailwind.
insights.quote: may be the work of a coordinated Kremlin campaign launched from Russian-linked commercial ships

insights.claim: IISS identified 144 drone sightings over Europe unlikely to be hobbyist or Ukraine-war related, with ~48% over military bases, 26% over critical infrastructure such as ports and energy facilities, and 18% over civilian airports, mostly at night.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Quantifies the threat surface and shows infrastructure/airports are primary targets, framing the addressable market for detection and neutralization systems.
insights.quote: About 48 percent of the sightings took place over military bases, 26 percent happened over critical infrastructure such as ports and energy or industrial facilities, and 18 percent occurred over civilian airports.
insights.locator: The IISS report identified 144 drone sightings

insights.claim: IISS characterizes the incursion pattern as deliberately calibrated to stay below the threshold of an armed attack, probing allied defenses and normalizing low-level airspace violations rather than causing direct damage.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Gray-zone framing means Western retaliation is legally constrained, so the threat persists and demand for non-kinetic counter-drone and legal/rules-of-engagement solutions grows.
insights.quote: consistent with the Kremlin's effort to probe allied defenses, test civilian-military response mechanisms and normalize low-level airspace violations below the threshold of an armed attack.
insights.locator: The think tank's report does not claim

insights.claim: The only drone incident directly attributed to Russia was a February 2026 launch from the Russian signals-intelligence vessel Zhigulevsk in Swedish waters, jammed by the Swedish military while the French carrier Charles de Gaulle was visiting nearby, proving Russian-linked ships can launch drones at sea.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Establishes confirmed ship-launch capability, validating the maritime-launch thesis even though most incidents remain circumstantial.
insights.locator: The only drone incident directly attributed to Russia

insights.claim: The Orlan-10 fixed-wing drone (500 km range, up to 12 hours endurance, 90-130 km/h) fits within a mid-sized commercial vessel's deck space, making shadow-fleet oil tankers viable stand-off launch platforms operating beyond visual range of European coasts.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Commercial tankers as covert drone carriers make attribution and interdiction extremely hard, raising the value of maritime-domain-awareness and AIS-analytics providers.
insights.quote: consistent with maritime launch from a vessel operating well beyond visual detection range of the European coastlines in question
insights.locator: A more common Russian drone model

insights.claim: French commandos boarding the oil tanker Boracay off France on Sept 28, 2025 found a Chinese captain plus two Russians from the Moran Security Group (a PMC founded by ex-FSB officers, one a former Wagner employee) tasked with intelligence-gathering and ensuring adherence to Russian interests.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: This is IISS's cited 'direct evidence' linking a shadow-fleet vessel to Russian intelligence structures, hardening the attribution case and the sanctions/enforcement rationale.
insights.quote: gathering intelligence, protecting the vessel and ensuring the captain strictly adhered to Russian interests.
insights.locator: The French commandos found that the ship

insights.claim: The Boracay was investigated by Danish authorities after September 2025 drone sightings disrupted air traffic across Denmark and forced temporary closure of Copenhagen Airport and the Aalborg Royal Danish Air Force base.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Demonstrates airport-closure disruption as the tangible economic cost, sharpening the case for airport counter-drone deployment spending.
insights.locator: One of the most intriguing ship suspects

insights.claim: Dutch drone incursions in November 2025 coincided with shadow-fleet ships Arctica, Cgas Leopard, Tranquil Sea, and Eagle S loitering near Dutch and French coasts; the Eagle S tanker had earlier been probed for severing Baltic undersea cables on Christmas 2024, though a Finnish court dismissed the case for lack of jurisdiction.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Shows repeat-offender vessels spanning cable-cutting and drone launches, and a legal-jurisdiction gap that lets them operate with impunity.
insights.locator: Those drone incursions over the Netherlands coincided

insights.claim: A French court sentenced the Boracay's Chinese captain in absentia on March 30, 2026 to one year in prison plus a $172,000 fine for failing to comply with orders to stop his ship.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Illustrates the weak, symbolic legal deterrent currently available against shadow-fleet crews, reinforcing that enforcement capacity is the binding constraint.

insights.claim: IISS names the Merlin-VR, a Russian fixed-wing drone launchable by shipboard catapult and recoverable by parachute with night-operation and loiter capability, as a candidate for ship-launched incursions.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Catapult-launch/parachute-recovery designs confirm purpose-built maritime drone tech exists, informing which defense suppliers and counter-measures matter.
insights.locator: One possible drone candidate for ship-launched operations

insights.claim: Russia has developed VTOL drones such as the Legioner E29 needing minimal deck space, and IISS warns homemade or modified commercial drones may be used specifically to prevent easy attribution to Russia.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Deliberate use of off-the-shelf hardware for deniability means attribution stays murky and defensive systems must handle a heterogeneous drone fleet.

insights.claim: The Orlan-10 can carry a GPS/GNSS-spoofing module, a communications-network monitoring module, and optical and thermal sensors for stand-off collection against coastal and inland targets.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: GPS-spoofing and SIGINT payloads mean the drones are electronic-warfare and surveillance assets, not just nuisances, widening the counter-EW investment case.
insights.locator: The Orlan-10 range and payload capabilities

insights.claim: Drone incursions repeatedly targeted NATO nuclear-linked bases including RAF Lakenheath, Kleine-Brogel (Belgium), Volkel (Netherlands), and Ile Longue (France, housing French ballistic-missile submarines), indicating deliberate targeting of nuclear-sharing and deterrent assets.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Targeting of nuclear infrastructure escalates the strategic stakes and political pressure for rapid counter-drone procurement at hardened sites.
insights.locator: The drone incidents over the UK military bases

insights.claim: RAF Lakenheath, the largest US military base in the UK, is set to receive more than $1.6 billion in upgrades including facilities for housing a nuclear arsenal.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Concentrates high-value nuclear infrastructure spend at a repeatedly probed site, a prime candidate for base-perimeter counter-drone contracts.
insights.locator: As the largest US military base in the UK

insights.claim: On November 21, 2025 a 'highly coordinated' swarm of up to 10 drones over Volkel Air Base evaded capture despite base security attempts to shoot them down, while coinciding sightings over Eindhoven Airport forced a brief shutdown of civil air traffic across the southern Netherlands.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Demonstrated failure to neutralize a coordinated swarm exposes a capability gap and validates urgent demand for effective kinetic/non-kinetic counter-swarm systems.
insights.locator: The biggest drone incursion at Volkel

insights.claim: The cargo ship Hav Dolphin (Antigua-and-Barbuda-flagged, Russian crew) was docked in the UK during the November 2024 Lakenheath incursions and later anchored near Kiel in May 2025 coinciding with sightings at the German submarine base at Eckernforde.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A single vessel correlating with drone events in two countries strengthens the specific-ship attribution pattern that maritime tracking analytics can surface.
insights.locator: The drone incidents over the UK military bases in November 2024 occurred

insights.claim: The European response to the drone campaign has been fragmented and uncoordinated, with the EU's European Drone Defence Initiative (EDDI) for interoperable detection and neutralization not expected to be fully functional until the end of 2027.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A multi-year capability gap through 2027 creates a sustained procurement window for counter-drone vendors and highlights near-term European vulnerability.

insights.claim: IISS argues hardware alone is insufficient without political authority to use it, urging European governments to establish legal clarity around rules of engagement for drone incursions.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: The binding constraint is political/legal, not technological, so watch EU rules-of-engagement policy as the real unlock for counter-drone deployment.
insights.quote: no amount of hardware will compensate for the absence of political authority to use it

insights.claim: IISS frames maritime accountability as the 'hardest' problem, requiring European governments to be far more willing to investigate and stop Russian-linked and shadow-fleet vessels loitering near coasts and launching drones with effective impunity.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Points to stronger maritime interdiction and sanctions enforcement of the shadow fleet as the highest-leverage policy lever, with knock-on effects for Russian oil logistics.
